coredata.dc:title: Multidimensional nanochannel design and regulation of ultra-thin GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Hao
Zhao, Wei
Liu, Hailiang
Sun, Yumin
Xiong, Huanyi
Huang, Qinglin
Chen, Kaikai
coredata.dc:description: Graphene-based composite membranes with long and tortuous transport paths restricted its efficient and wide application in substance separation and purification field. In this study, we propose a multidimensional mass-transfer channel construction method for fabricating highly permeable 2D laminar membranes with lots of stable molecular transport channels. In particular, a robust GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes with adjustable interlayer sieving channels were prepared based on vacuum assisted self-assembly by intercalating AGQDs with average size of 7.5 nm between GOQDs with average size of 34.0 nm, which presented highly permeability with plentiful nano-channels in both vertical and horizontal directions. As the mass fraction of doped AGQDs reached to 60 wt%, the best performance of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes was obtained, with a functional layer thickness of 24.1 nm, good stability, and strong acid and alkali resistance. The usage of small diameter GOQDs with intercalating smaller diameter AGQDs extremely shortened the transfer channel path and slightly increased the interlayer spacing (8.26 to 8.75 Å), which dramatically improved its permeation flux of 14 times higher than that of the pure GO membrane with similar dye rejection, and reached to 133.3 L·m−2·h−1 under 2 bar at the optimized condition. This study provided insights into self-assembling small-sized 2D nanomaterials into membranes and fine-tuning their nanochannel structure.

head.ce:title: Multidimensional nanochannel design and regulation of ultra-thin GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hao Cui and Wei Zhao are the co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene-based composite membranes with long and tortuous transport paths restricted its efficient and wide application in substance separation and purification field. In this study, we propose a multidimensional mass-transfer channel construction method for fabricating highly permeable 2D laminar membranes with lots of stable molecular transport channels. In particular, a robust GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes with adjustable interlayer sieving channels were prepared based on vacuum assisted self-assembly by intercalating AGQDs with average size of 7.5 nm between GOQDs with average size of 34.0 nm, which presented highly permeability with plentiful nano-channels in both vertical and horizontal directions. As the mass fraction of doped AGQDs reached to 60 wt%, the best performance of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes was obtained, with a functional layer thickness of 24.1 nm, good stability, and strong acid and alkali resistance. The usage of small diameter GOQDs with intercalating smaller diameter AGQDs extremely shortened the transfer channel path and slightly increased the interlayer spacing (8.26 to 8.75 Å), which dramatically improved its permeation flux of 14 times higher than that of the pure GO membrane with similar dye rejection, and reached to 133.3 L·m−2·h−1 under 2 bar at the optimized condition. This study provided insights into self-assembling small-sized 2D nanomaterials into membranes and fine-tuning their nanochannel structure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials, as a kind of building block with nanoporous structures, can be stacked to form interlayer structures with sub-nanometer, and effective separation of various molecules can be achieved by molecular sieving and electrostatic interactions [1,2]. In particular, graphene with a 2D structure and its graphene derivatives, such as graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have good physicochemical properties with an ultra-thin thickness (usually 1–2 layers of carbon atoms thick), nearly frictionless surface, easy to modify and excellent mechanical strength [3], which facilitated its wide application in the field of pervaporation [4], gas separation [5,6], water purification [7] and energy storage [8], etc. These materials may be readily synthesized in large quantities, and membranes comprising them can be fabricated on porous substrates by various scalable solution-based techniques, making them highly desirable for use as membrane materials [9].

Even with the aforementioned benefits of 2D graphene derivatives in the field of membranes, the extensive and convoluted water permeation channels of 2D graphene-based laminar membranes lead to extremely poor permeation performance. This resemblance result has been confirmed by Yang et al. [10], wherein they demonstrated an ultra-low water flux and nearly impermeable organic liquids, such hexane and methanol, upon deposition of highly laminated GO sheets with a thickness exceeding 100 nm. Therefore, the 2D graphene-based laminar membrane can be improved by the acceptable design of the interlayer channel and pore size to achieve a high permeation flux with a high rejection effect [11]. Generally, water and ions traverse 2D graphene-based laminar membranes during the separation process via the following routes: horizontal nano-channels, which are formed by 2D intercalation between adjacent nanosheets; and vertical nano-channels, which are formed by structural defects generated on the nanosheet surface or existing edge space between adjacent nanosheets [12–14].

One of the effective ways to improve membrane flux is to regulate the horizontal nano-channels, which can employ nanomaterials to modify the interlayer spacing of 2D graphene-based laminar membranes [15]. These nanomaterials generally encompass several component types, such as tiny organic molecules [16,17], 2D nanosheets [18,19], and inorganic nanoparticles [20,21]. Yan et al. [21] used sodium borate tetrahydrate pentaborate (STB) as an intercalation material to modulate the sub-nanometer space between GO nanosheets. By varying the concentration of STB, the surface chemistry and interlayer spacing of the membrane were modulated, thus improving the permeability and dye rejection performance compared to pure GO membrane. Wei et al. [19] created 2D GO/MXene composite membranes by combining 2D GO nanosheets with 2D MXene nanosheets, which varied the doping percentage of MXene nanosheets to control the interlayer channels via vacuum filtering method. However, some significant flaws exist in these 2D graphene-based laminar membranes. For example, while expanding the layer spacing of the laminar membrane can improve its permeability to some extent, it is often difficult to retain some small molecules and reduce its selectivity. Alternatively, if the defects are solved by thickening, the permeability of the membrane will be reduced due to the increased water molecule transport route [22]. These methods cannot overcome the trade-off effect between selectivity and permeability prevalent existed in membrane materials.

Another efficient way to increase the permeability of 2D graphene-based laminar membranes is to increase the vertical nano-channels, which can realize by creating tunable pore density and pore size on the surface of 2D graphene-based materials [22,23]. Increasing the number of vertical nano-channels is thought to be a promising technique for particularly customizing the porosity of 2D graphene-based laminar membranes. The preparation of nanopores in 2D materials includes several approaches such as directional recombination [24,25], physical etching [26,27], and liquid phase oxidation [28,29]. Li et al. [29] generated in-plane nanopores on GO nanosheets via H2O2 oxidation. These nanosheets were subsequently vacuum-filtered into a 2D laminar membranes, which exhibited strong water permeability and consistent divalent ion rejection. Recently, it has been shown that the 2D laminar membranes assembled from small-sized 2D GO nanosheets (average size of 100–200 nm) can achieve improved permeability of GO membranes by reducing the transport channel length [30]. Smaller GO sheets can provide shorter and less tortuous transport channels, which will be a good way to increase the route number of molecules through the membrane while maintaining shorter transport paths for transported molecules by starting with the modulation of the horizontal and vertical nano-channels of the 2D laminar membranes.

As nanoscale fragments of 2D GO sheets, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with nanosheets smaller than 100 nm exhibit graphene's inherent features as well as special physical/chemical capabilities (e.g., higher edge-to-volume ratio, abundant edge groups, excellent dispersion, chemical stability, etc.) [31–36]. Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) nanosheets had the same oxygen-containing groups as GO nanosheets, while amino graphene quantum dots (AGQDs) had oxygen-containing groups as well as a large number of reactive amine groups at their borders [24,33]. When the GOQDs nanosheets were assembled into a membrane from its dispersive solution, the π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding between the nanosheets could guide their self-assembly behavior due to the abundance of oxygen −containing functional groups at their edges [45,46,48–50]. When embedded in AGQDs nanosheets, the –NH2 groups on the edges of AGQDs nanosheets are able to form covalent bonding connections, electrostatic mutual attraction, and hydrogen bonding interactions with the –COOH groups on GOQDs nanosheets. These interactions would contribute to the construction of interlayer nano-channels. The two transport channels of the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane can then be controlled simultaneously, with the vertical nano-channels being co-regulated by GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets to provide more transport channels, while the addition of AGQDs nanosheets regulates the interlayer spacing of the horizontal nano-channels.

Herein, a kind of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes was prepared based on vacuum assisted self-assembly technique by embedding AGQDs nanosheets (average size: 7.5 nm) between GOQDs nanosheets (average size: 34.0 nm), which constructed the effective multidimensional mass-transfer channels both horizontally and vertically. Then the nano-transport channels of the prepared GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes in vertical and horizontal directions were fine-tuned by varying the content of AGQDs nanosheets. The effect of this regulation on the pore structure of the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes was analyzed in detail. In addition, the corresponding chemical structure, surface properties, morphology and dye separation of the self-assembled GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes were specifically investigated.

GO (monolayer ratio > 98 %) was originated from Soochow Heng qiu Graphene Technology Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Polyethersulfone (PES) UF membrane (pore size: 0.10 μm) was provided from Haiyan Xin Dong Fang Technology Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) UF membrane (pore size: 0.22 μm) was supplied by Labfilter Co. Ltd. (USA). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) and Na2SO4 were acquired from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ammonia (NH3·H2O, 25 ∼ 28 wt%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid(36.5 %), Congo Red (CR), Evans Blue (EB) and Direct Red 80 (DR) were obtained from Kermel Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

GOQDs were generated through a “top-down” chemical cleavage of GO utilizing H2O2 [33,37,38]. Overall, 100 mL of aqueous GO solution was prepared at a concentration of 0.2 w/v%. Subsequently, 100 ml of H2O2 (30 wt%) was added to the solution. The two were combined extensively before being stripped ultrasonically for three hours. After removing excess H2O2 by stirring, condensing, and refluxing the aqueous solution of the reacted mixture at 100 °C for four hours, it was filtered through a PTFE filter membrane to eliminate sizable particles. At 5 °C, the acquired GOQDs solution was refrigerated.

AGQD were prepared by the addition reaction of chemically cracked monolayer GO with ammonia nucleophilic addition. [24,35]. Ammonia was used to break the epoxy bond on GO and add the amino group when breaking GO in order to create the AGQDs. Specifically, ammonia was mixed with an aqueous GO solution (0.1 w/v%) in a 1:3 ratio. Next, the mixture was heated to 120 °C for 5 h in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The obtained light yellow solution was put into a round-neck flask and put through a decompression distillation process (85 °C, 1 h) with magnetic stirring to get rid of the extra water and ammonia. Once the mixture was cool, it was filtered through a PTFE filter membrane. Finally, the obtained AGQDs solution was stored in a fridge at 5 °C.

First, a GOQDs aqueous solution with the concentration of 0.01 w/v% was prepared and treated under ultrasonic condition for 30 min. Then, different volumes of GOQDs aqueous were drew out and mixed with 50 mL of distilled water under ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Next, the GOQDs aqueous solutions with varying contents were pumped onto the surface of PES ultrafiltration membranes under a negative pressure of 3 bar. Finally, the GOQDs/PES composite membranes were heat-treated in a vacuum oven at 45 °C for 10 min. The resulted membranes were named as GM (0–7), where the numbers represented the various loadings (0.00, 28.5, 57.0, 86.0, 114.0, 143.0, 286.0, 429.0, respectively, in mg∙m−2).

Different from the GOQDs/PES composite membranes, the GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes were prepared used the GOQDs and AGQDs mixed aqueous solutions instead of the GOQDs aqueous solutions. In order to maintain the consistency of membrane forming performance, the total solution content was preferred to be 1.0 mL with the total loading of 143.0 mg∙m−2 based on the above experiment, where the mass fraction of AGQDs was 0.0 wt%, 20 wt%, 40 wt%, 60 wt%, 80 wt%, 100 wt%, respectively. Then, the GOQDs-AGQDs/PES composite membranes were produced according to the above steps. The resulted membranes were named as GM5-(A1-A5) and the numbers indicated different AGQDs mass fractions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GOQDs, AGQDs and membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The sizes of GOQDs and AGQDs were determined using transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi H7650, TEM, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, ThermoFisher, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Fisher, USA) were used to characterize the surface functional groups and elemental contents of GOQDs, AGQDs, and membranes, respectively. Cold field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, S4800, Hitachi, Japan), equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) detector was used to characterize characterize the surface, cross-sectional morphology, and elemental distribution of the membranes, respectively. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Brooke Icon, USA) was utilized to assess the membrane surface roughness. An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 DISCOVER, Germany) was used to measure the membranes' interlayer spacing. The pore size and pore size distribution of the membranes were supplied by the BET pore size analyzer (BSD-PM2, China) with CO2 adsorption isotherm using DFT method. Water contact angle and surface potential were measured using a water contact angle meter (Kruss DSA-100, Germany) and a solid surface zeta potential analyzer (SURPASS-3, Anton Paar, Austria).

At room temperature and 2 bar pressure, an in-lab cross-flow filtration apparatus was used to assess the prepared membranes separation performance. For the pure dye rejection test, 0.1 g∙L-1 of CR, EB, and DR were prepared. Subsequently, a mixed system consisting of 1.0 g∙L-1 of Na2SO4 solution and 0.1 g∙L-1 of the aforementioned dyes was prepared to assess the dye desalination performance. The membrane with an effective area of 7 cm2 was stabilized at 2 bar for 20 min. and the membrane permeate flux (J) and rejection rate (R) were then obtained from Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)
J =
V
T × A
Where, J was the permeation flux (L·m−2·h−1); V, T, and A were the quantity of permeate(L), the test time(h), and the effective area of the membrane (m2), respectively.
(2)
R =
C f
-
C p
C f
∗ 100 %
Where, C f and C p were the feed concentration and permeate concentration, respectively. Dye concentration and divalent salt solution concentration were indirectly determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1810) and conductivity meter (OHAUS ST3100C), respectively.

In order to evaluate the stability of the membranes, the prepared composite membranes were tested for filtration performance over a long period of time in the CR/Na2SO4 mixed system. The flux and rejection rate were recorded every 1 h after 24 h of continuous operation at 2 bar and calculated using the Eqs. (1) and (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and properties of GOQDs and AGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets was demonstrated in Fig. 1 a. GOQDs were prepared mainly by attacking the epoxy group on top of GO by the dissociated ·OH radicals from H2O2, as well as effective oxidative cleavage by the combined effect of ultrasonic waves, which picked up more –COOH and –OH at the fracture edges [33,37–39]. AGQDs were prepared by cleaving the epoxy group on GO by nucleophilic substitution reaction using the amino group in NH3·H2O, which attached abundant –NH2 and –OH groups at the fracture edges [24,35,40].

TEM images could accurately represent the specific size and morphological structure of GO, GOQDs, and AGQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the GO sheet diameters displayed micrometer-level sizes. The size of GOQDs was roughly ranged in 26.0–40.0 nm (average size:34.0 nm), whereas the size of AGQDs was ranged in 5.5–9.0 nm (average size: 7.5 nm), as demonstrated in Fig. 2b, c. It was strongly supported that the big-sized GO was divided into small-sized GOQDs and AGQDs by H2O2 and NH3·H2O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2d, GOQDs and AGQDs aqueous solutions displayed yellowish fluorescence and light blue fluorescence phenomena, respectively, when exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm. This suggested that the two types of GQDs had good dispersion, small size, and specific activity in water.

The chemical structure composition of GO, GOQDs and AGQDs were characterized using ATR-FTIR. As shown in Fig. 2e, both of the GO and GOQDs showed certain stretching vibration peaks, which included the hydroxyl (–OH) group at around 3300 cm−1, the carbonyl (C = O) group at about 1718 cm−1, the aromatic olefin (C = C) group at 1625 cm−1 [33,41]. The difference was that the epoxy group (C-O-C) at 1220 cm−1 characteristic peaks on GOQDs almost disappeared compared to GO, while the intensity of the C = O and –OH groups characteristic peaks increased, indicating the formation of many oxygen containing groups on the GOQDs edges. For the FTIR spectra of AGQDs, the typical amine group was appeared, including the stretching vibration of –NH at 3200–3500 and 799 cm−1, the bending vibration of –NH at 1580 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of C-N at 1075 cm−1 [42,43]. These new characteristic peaks proved beyond any doubt that AGQDs had more –NH2 active groups.

XPS analyses were performed to further confirm the presence of oxygenic functional groups on the edges of GOQDs and amino groups on the edges of AGQDs. As shown in Fig. 2f, the O/C of GOQDs (0.59) was significantly higher than that of GO (0.49), suggesting that GOQDs contained more oxygen-containing groups. Compared with GO, the peak area of GOQDs at C-O (286.8 eV) was considerably diminished, whereas was considerably increased at C = O (288.3 eV) from Fig. 2g and h. From Fig. 2j and k, the C = O (532.3 eV) peak area in GOQDs was nearly double that of GO, whereas the decrease in C-OH/C-O-C (533.3 eV) peak area was caused by the breakage of the epoxy bond, introducing more –OH and –COOH [33]. In contrast to GO, the C-O signal at 286.78 eV in AGQDs was significantly weakened from Fig. 2i. The nucleophilic substitution process between GO and ammonia produced a large number of reactive –NH2 at AGQDs nanosheet margins, which further demonstrated the presence of C-N and –NH2 signals at 400.8 eV and 398.9 eV in the N1s spectrum from Fig. 2l, respectively [43,44]. The above findings fully demonstrated that the large pieces of GO were dissociated into small pieces of carboxyl-rich GOQDs and amino-rich AGQDs under the action of H2O2 and NH3·H2O, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S1, the series of GOQDs composite membranes' morphology and surface roughness were displayed. As the loading of GOQDs increased, the surface pores of PES support membrane were gradually covered, and the thickness of the functional layer increased, while the surface roughness decreased. As shown in Fig. S2, when the GOQDs loading was higher than 86.0 mg·m−2, the CR rejection could be maintained at a high level, but the flux was low. The high loading led to an increase in membrane thickness, which was not conducive to the improvement of flux. For comprehensive consideration, the dosage of GM5 membrane was determined as the dosage of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes. Fig. 3 showed the morphological structure and roughness of the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes, which formed by GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets with different mass fractions at the same loading. When the mass fraction of AGQDs was less than 60 wt%, the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes showed a defect-free functional layer with a slight decrease in roughness. This was due to the crosslinking and electrostatic attraction between GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets, while the reactive groups (e.g., amino and carboxyl groups) on GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets could also form hydrogen bonding interactions with the oxygen-containing functional groups on the PES, which resulted in the assembly of GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets onto the PES supporting membranes. As the mass fraction of AGQDs increased, the thickness of the membranes decreased slightly (from 27.2 nm to 23.6 nm) from Fig. 3a2–c2. The HTEM photograph of the cross-section of the GM5-A3 membrane showed a functional layer thickness of 24.1 nm from Fig. 4 f, which was consistent with the FESEM results. The formation of low surface roughness and ultra-thin separation layer thickness would contribute to increased permeation flux [47].

However, when the mass fraction of AGQDs was higher than 60 wt%, the surface pores of the support membrane started to be exposed and the roughness increased, while the separation layer thickness of GM5-A5 membrane could not be accurately measured. The phenomenon was caused by following two reasons. Firstly, the reduction of GOQDs content reduced the –COOH content in the mixed solutions, thereby weakening the interactions (including electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding and covalent bonding) between GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets. Secondly, the reduction of GOQDs content resulted in the small-sized AGQDs not being able to fall all onto the support membrane, which could be illustrated by comparing the fluorescence colors of the mixed solution of GOQDs and AGQDs before and after filtration (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemical properties of GOQDs- AGQDs membranes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the changes in the functional groups of composite membrane after the addition of AGQDs, the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes were characterized using FTIR and XPS, respectively. A careful observation revealed that the peak of the –OH characteristic at 3500–3200 cm−1 was significantly shifted to low wavelengths after the addition of AGQDs compared with the GM5 membrane without AGQDs. Meanwhile, the weak amide II peaks of the N-H bending vibration and the C-N stretching vibration at 1542 cm−1 indicated the acylation reaction between the –NH2 on the AGQDs and the –COOH on the GOQDs [51]. From Fig. 4b, the GM5-A3 membrane showed the characteristic peak of elemental nitrogen at 400 eV compared to the GM5 membrane (without AGQDs), which indicated the successful embedding of AGQDs into the functional layer. It was worth mentioning that no S elements were detected, indicating the formation of the defect-free functional layer for the GM5-A3 membrane. From the C1s spectrum of the GM5-A3 membrane in Fig. 4d, four peaks could be identified, including C = C/C-C (284.9 eV), C-N (286.3 eV), C-O (286.9 eV), and C = O (288.3 eV), which the C-N peaks appeared in the GM5-A3 membrane compared with the GM5 membrane. In order to understand the state of nitrogen element in the GM5-A3 membrane, the split-peak fitting of nitrogen was performed and was shown in Fig. 4e. The nitrogen peak could be divided into three peaks, namely –NH3 + (401.1 eV), N-C = O (399.9 eV) and –NH2 (398.8 eV), which demonstrated the successful reaction between GOQDs and AGQDs [52,53]. The EDS test revealed that the N element was spread out evenly on the GM5-A3 membrane (Fig. 4g), indicating that the AGQDs were successfully incorporated into the functional layer.

The surface zeta potential of the membranes under different conditions was shown in Fig. 5 a. When no AGQDs were incorporated, the deprotonation of the –COOH caused the GM5 membrane to display a higher negative charge [54,55]. As the mass fraction of AGQDs increased, the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes showed lower negative charge. This change was mainly due to the reaction of the –COOH on the GOQDs with the –NH2 on the AGQDs, which consumed the –COOH to form an amide bond. Secondly, unreacted –NH2 on AGQDs formed NH3 + in solution, inhibiting the ionization of –COOH. In addition, the prepared GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane displayed negative charge in the solution environment of pH = 7, which produced better repulsion of anionic dyes in solution.

All of the composite membranes showed hydrophilic characteristics (Fig. S4). The contact angle of the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes decreased slightly with the increase in the mass fraction of AGQDs and then increased again. Due to the presence of hydrophilic groups (–COOH, –NH2 and –OH), AGQDs-GOQDs composite membranes could also exhibit small contact angle when the mass fraction of AGQDs was less than 60 wt%. When the mass fraction of AGQDs was higher than 60 wt%, the GOQDs and AGQDs constituting the functional layer could not completely fall on the support membrane, causing the surface structure of the support membrane to be partially exposed, which led to the reduction of hydrophilic groups on the surface of the composite membrane and the increase of contact angle.

For 2D graphene-based membranes, interlayer spacing (as a molecular transport path) is critical for separation performance. XRD was commonly used to characterize the size of the interlayer spacing between sheets in 2D graphene-based materials. As shown in Fig. 5b, GO, GOQDs and GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes showed peaks at 2θ = 10.7°, 11.1° and 10.1°, respectively. High-intensity diffraction peaks were observed on both pure GO and GOQD composite membranes, suggesting that the nanosheets stacked together in a more regular manner during the pumping process [56]. Nevertheless, the intensity of the diffraction peaks was weakened by the addition of AGQDs. This may be due to the interaction between GOQDs and AGQDs affecting the stacking of the nanosheets, forming two stacking patterns corresponding to two peaks between GOQDs and between GOQDs and AGQDs. The superposition of the two peaks caused the decrease in the intensity of the diffraction peaks [57]. The corresponding interlayer spacing could be calculated using the Bragg equation. As shown in Fig. 5c, the interlayer spacing of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes was reached to 8.75 Å, which was larger than that of GO (8.26 Å) and GOQDs (7.89 Å) membranes. It indicated that small-sized AGQDs could not only interact with GOQDs in the horizontal direction, but also embed themselves between GOQDs nanosheets, thus enlarging the layer spacing.

In order to investigate the mechanism of the superior performance of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes over GOQDs membranes, we constructed the molecular model to simulate the free volume of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes (see the computational simulation process in SI). As shown in Fig. 5d and e, the free volume fraction of GM5-A3 (0.71) was larger than that of GM5 (0.58), revealing that the addition of AGQDs effectively increased the average porosity of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes. In addition, the pore size distribution of GM5 and GM5-A3 were shown in Fig. 5f, while the adsorption isotherm was shown in Fig. S5. It was found that the pore size distribution of GM5 and GM5-A3 membranes was mainly concentrated at 0.4–0.7 nm, and the pore size of GM5-A3 membrane was increased slightly from 0.501 nm to 0.573 nm after the 60 wt% addition of AGQDs, which was close to the pore size of GO laminar membranes in the literatures [75]. From the above analysis, it could be concluded that different mass fractions of AGQDs could fine-tune the charge, hydrophilicity, layer spacing and pore size of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes, thus affecting their permeation properties.

Here, in order to investigate the differences between GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes and conventional 2D graphene-based membranes, we chose to study the nano-channels of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane compared with GO membrane. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, conventional GO membrane had few surface defects, and water molecules could only pass through a small number of voids formed by nearby nanosheets. On the other hand, the GO nanosheets with large sheet diameter generally formed a tortuous transport channel. For GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes, the combination of small-sized GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets not only increased the number of voids between GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets, but also expanded the interlayer spacing for horizontal passage of water molecules, while reducing the tortuosity of the vertical water transport channels. Among them, the composition of the multidimensional nanochannels mainly included two types of horizontal passages and three types of vertical passages, which the horizontal passages consisted of layer spacing between GOQDs nanosheets (7.89 Å) and layer spacing between GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets (8.75 Å), while the vertical passages consisted of the pores between adjacent GOQDs, AGQDs, GOQDs and AGQDs. These two types of horizontal passages and three types of vertical passages were interleaved so as to form water molecule transport channels in multidimensional directions. Thus, the presence of this multidimensional mass-transfer channel structure in both vertical and horizontal directions could significantly improve the permeation performance of the membranes.

The dye rejection properties of the prepared GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes were displayed in Fig. 7 a. The relative high CR rejection (96.3 %) and high permeation flux were maintained as the mass fraction of AGQDs was less than 60 wt%. Then the CR rejection was significant decreased with the further increase in AGQDs mass fraction, which was closely related to the incompleteness of the functional layer on the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane surface from Fig. 3. Therefore, as the mass fraction of doped AGQDs reached to 60 wt%, the obtained GM5-A3 membranes exhibited the best comprehensive performance.

In order to evaluate the dye separation performance of GM5-A3 membrane, the different types of dyes as feed solutions were employed to investigate its filtration performance. As shown in Fig. 7b, the GM5-A3 membrane inhibited dyes such as EB and DR by more than 99 %. The dye separation was based on spatial repulsion effect and Donnan repulsion effect. Firstly, the dye particle size of EB and DR was larger than CR, and secondly, the negative charge on the surface of GM5-A3 membrane endowed it a better rejection effect on anionic dyes.

Compared to GO and GOQDs composite membranes in Fig. 7c, the optimized GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes (GM5-A3, 133.3 L·m−2·h−1) provided 4 times the permeation flux of GOQDs membranes (34.8 L·m−2·h−1) and even 14 times that of GO membranes (9.8 L·m−2·h−1), while maintaining a relatively high CR rejection. These results showed that although the composite membrane consisting of small-sized GOQDs had more vertical nano-channels, the layer spacing was slightly reduced, which the permeation performance showed only several times higher than that of GO membranes under the same action of hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. When the appropriate amount of AGQDs was introduced, the acylation reactions and interactions between –NH2 on AGQDs and –COOH on GOQDs would guide the arrangement of GOQDs/AGQDs nanosheets to fine-tune the structure of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes. The GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes with larger pore size and layer spacing were finally obtained, which greatly improved its permeation performance compared with GO membranes. Fig. 7d showed the tolerance of GM5-A3 membrane in strong acid and alkali solutions at pH = 0 and pH = 14, which showed the unchanged CR rejection and slightly decreased permeation flux for the GM5-A3 membrane, indicating that the GOQDs-AGQDs functional layer had good resistance to strong acid and alkali. This was mainly attributed to the inherent properties of the membrane components (PES, GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets) and the crosslinking structure between them. First, the PES supporting membrane with benzene ring and sulfide ether bond itself possessed excellent acid and alkali resistance, which made it highly stable in harsh environments [82]. Second, the GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets themselves had excellent acid and alkali resistance stability [83]. Finally, the presence of certain “crosslinking” between GOQDs and AGQDs reduced the presence of oxygen atoms, while the formation of amide bonds ensured stability in aqueous solutions, which was consistent with FTIR spectroscopy and XPS characterization.

In the practical applications, the different dye/salt (CR/Na2SO4, EB/Na2SO4 and DR/Na2SO4) mixture solutions were used to simulate actual saline dye wastewater to investigate the dye desalination performance of GM5-A3 membrane. As shown in Fig. 7e, the dye rejection of GM5-A3 membrane for the CR/Na2SO4, EB/Na2SO4 and DR/Na2SO4 mixture solutions reached to 95.6 %, 96.0 % and 96.1 %, while the Na2SO4 rejection were 23.6 %, 36.5 % and 24.0 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6, the rejection of dyes (CR, EB, and DR) by GO membrane during dye desalination was maintained above 96.5 %. Compared to the GM5-A3 membrane, the permeation flux of the GO membrane was drastically reduced while maintaining similar rejection. These dyes rejection performance were slightly lower compared to the pure dye solution from Fig. 7b, but still more than 95 % in the separation of saline dye wastewater. Conversely, the permeation fluxes were nearly 20 % higher than those in pure dye solutions at the operating pressure of 2 bar, which were 175.5 L·m−2·h−1, 190.6 L·m−2·h−1, and 184.22 L·m−2·h−1, respectively. This may be related to the charge amount carried by different dye molecule, with EB and DR dyes carrying more negatively charged sodium sulfonate groups than CR dyes. At a certain salt concentration, the Dannon repulsion effect was weakened, which reduced the repulsion of the anionic dyes, but the size sieving effect still worked. As a result, the dye rejection decreased slightly under both effects, while flux appeared to increase [58]. In addition, due to the excellent permeability of GM5-A3 membrane, it was expected to be further applied to other liquid separation fields, such as wastewater treatment [76], water purification [77] and recovery of bioactives [78].

The dye performance of GM5-A3 membrane was compared with the representative GO and its modified membranes in the literature (Fig. 7f and Table S1). These GO based composite membranes mainly used increasing the size of interlayer nano-channels to improve its permeation performance. Distinguishing from the reported methods in the literature, the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes in this study were designed to increase the number of vertical nano-channels by decreasing the size of the sheet diameters and to reduce the tortuosity of the mass-transfer channels, meanwhile moderately enlarging the size of the horizontal nano-channels, thus achieving a significant improvement in the permeability. The results showed that the GM5-A3 exhibited excellent permeability and comparable dye rejection properties, which indicated its potential application in practical dye separation.

To evaluate the circulating stability of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes, the circulating dye desalination tests were performed under cross-flow filtration. After running 1 h at 0.2 MPa, the membrane sample was continued to be rinsed with pure water for 30 min to recover its permeability, which was a cycle, and a total of 4 cycles were carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 a, after 4 cycles, the GM5-A3 membrane exhibited a stable high CR rejection (above 95 %), despite the permeate flux decreased, which indicated that the GM5-A3 membrane had good stability and certain anti-fouling performance.

Furthermore, the long-term stability of obtained GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes were performed in the CR/Na2SO4 mixture system, which the membrane samples were continuously tested for 24 h at 0.2 MPa and recorded every 1 h. As shown in Fig. 8b, with the extension of the experimental time, the CR and Na2SO4 rejections of GM5-A3 membranes were slightly increased in long-term filtration, while the permeation flux was decreased more. It was possible that the functional layers stacked by GOQDs and AGQDs nanosheets had relatively large interlayer channels and pores, leading to the deposition of dye molecules inside the functional layer, which reduced the permeation flux.

To further evaluate the stability of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes in aqueous solution, the GO, GOQDs and GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes were immersed in deionized water for a period of time. As shown in Fig. S7, after soaking for up to 30 days, obvious defects appeared on the surface of the GO membrane, and certain defects also appeared on the surface of the GOQDs membrane, while the surface of the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane always maintained good integrity. Generally, GO and its derivatives composite membranes usually suffered from the problem of interlayer stability during the separation process. Water or solvent molecules entered into the GO interlayer during long time immersion, thus expanding the interlayer spacing, making the structure of composite membrane loose, and further significant shedding [65]. Usually, the stability of these composite membrane could be improved by reduction [79], interlayer crosslinking [80] and post-crosslinking [81]. For the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane, the –NH2 on the AGQDs could connect with the –COOH on the GOQDs by forming amide covalent bonds and electrostatic attraction, thereby effectively inhibited the swelling of the GOQDs-AGQDs functional layer in water and improved the stability of the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membrane.

In conclusion, we successfully prepared GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes by embedding AGQDs nanosheets between GOQDs nanosheets using vacuum-assisted self-assembly. The multidimensional nano-transport channels of GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes were modulated by embedding AGQDs nanosheets with different mass fractions. Highly permeable nanochannels with stable structure, adjustable pore size and interlayer spacing as well as regular morphology were constructed in both vertical and horizontal directions. When the mass fraction of embedded AGQDs was 60 wt%, the GM5-A3 membrane exhibited the best performance with a functional layer thickness of 24.1 nm, good stability, and strong acid and alkali resistance. The use of small diameter GOQDs with intercalating smaller diameter AGQDs significantly shortened the transfer channel path and slightly increased the interlayer spacing (8.26 to 8.75 Å), resulting in a permeation flux 13 times higher than that of pure GO membranes, meanwhile the GM5-A3 membrane reached a relative high permeation flux (175.5 L·m−2·h−1, 2 bar) with similar CR rejection (95.6%) and lower Na2SO4 rejection in the dye desalination experiment. Although the GOQDs-AGQDs composite membranes possessed high permeation performance, it had limited removal effect on small molecules, and if this problem could be solved, the future application of this membranes in the field of seawater desalination as well as gas separation would have great prospects. In brief, this work provided a new feasible method for the design and preparation of multidimensional mass-transfer channels for small-sized two-dimensional nanomaterials.

Hao Cui: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis. Wei Zhao: Methodology. Hailiang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yumin Sun: Investigation. Huanyi Xiong: Investigation. Qinglin Huang: Writing – review & editing. Kaikai Chen: Software.
